title: The Stablecoin Report That Changes Everything
transcript: Recently,  the  Financial  Action  Task   Force,  aka  the  FATF  F,  released  a   damning  report  suggesting  that  stable   coins  are  becoming  the  most  popular   means  of  payment  in  illicit  finance,  and   they're  calling  on  stable  coin  issuers   to  force  KYC  on  all  stable  coin  holders   and  wallet  towallet  transactions.  The   report  is  titled  quote  targeted  report   on  stable  coins  and  unhosted  wallets   peer-to-peer  transactions  and  we'll   leave  a  link  to  the  full  text  in  the   description.  Now,  the  report's  authors   start  by  explaining  that  the  first  US   dollar  stable  coin  launched  in  2014  and   since  then  the  stable  coin  space  has   grown  exponentially.  To  put  things  into   perspective,  there  were  259  different   stable  coins  circulating  at  the  end  of   July  last  year.  By  October  2025,  the   combined  market  cap  of  these  stable   coins  had  soared  to  $316  billion,  while   the  24-hour  trading  volume  for  the   sector  reached  $156  billion.  For   reference,  that's  roughly  three  times   higher  than  BTC.  95%  of  all  circulating   stable  coins  are  fiatbacked  with  97%  of   those  being  pegged  to  the  US  dollar.   Meanwhile,  just  3%  of  all  stable  coins   were  cryptobacked  and  only  0.2%  were   algorithmic  and  90%  of  all  stable  coins   were  centralized,  which  is  to  be   expected  given  the  volatility  of  the   alternatives.  Now,  the  report's  authors   note  that  the  reason  why  retail   investors,  institutional  investors,  and   virtual  asset  service  providers  or  VASPs   love  stable  coins  is  because  they  offer   multiple  advantages  including  trading,   near  instant  settlements,  low   transaction  fees,  crossber  payments,  and   247  uptime.  Stable  coins  are  also  used   as  a  store  of  value  by  those  living  in   countries  with  weak  currencies.  And  as   the  regulatory  backdrop  continues  to   improve  for  stable  coins  and  crypto  as  a   whole,  companies  are  becoming   increasingly  interested  in  adopting   stable  coins  into  their  key  business   models.  But  the  authors  note  that  more   bad  actors  are  using  stable  coins  for   illicit  activity,  too.  Specifically,   they  point  to  stablecoins  role  in  money   laundering  and  terrorist  financing.   According  to  research  from  chain   analysis,  84%  of  crypto-related  illicit   activity  involved  stable  coins.  That's   simply  because  they  offer  deep  liquidity   are  easy  to  use  crosschain  and  lack  the   volatility  of  other  cryptos  like  Bitcoin   for  instance.  I  guess  even  criminals   need  a  bit  of  stability.  And  while   different  criminal  groups  use  different   methods,  common  themes  include  relying   on  unlicensed  or  unregistered  VASPs  and   operating  outside  of  jurisdictions  that   enforce  AML,  that  is   anti-moneylaundering,  and  CFT,   countering  the  financing  of  terrorism   regulations.  Others  transact  OTC,  i.e.   over-the-counter,  or  use  tools  like   mixers  and  chain  hopping  to  obscure   their  illicit  activity.  And  now  before   we  go  any  further,  I  need  to  quickly   tell  you  about  our  weekly  newsletter.   That's  where  our  research  team  breaks   down  the  biggest  stories  shaping  the   market  and  highlights  key  catalysts  for   the  week  ahead.  Best  of  all,  it's   completely  free.  So  why  not  sign  up   using  the  link  below  or  by  scanning  this   QR  code.  And  now  back  to  the  report.   Okay,  so  the  next  part  of  the  FATF's   report  examines  the  main  groups  abusing   stable  coins,  starting  with  the   Democratic  People's  Republic  of  Korea  or   DPRK.   The  report's  authors  note  that  stable   coins  have  become  the  preferred  tool  for   state-  linked  cyber  criminals  laundering   funds  from  ransomware,  fishing  attacks,   and  other  illicit  activities.  They   highlight  the  massive  $1.4  4  billion   theft  from  major  crypto  exchange  Bybit   by  the  North  Korean  hacking  collective   known  as  the  Lazarus  Group  in  February   last  year.  And  that  money  was   subsequently  laundered  through  privacy   coins,  mixers,  crosschain  bridges,  and   over  125,000  Ethereum  wallets.  But   that's  just  one  example,  and  it's   arguably  not  even  the  worst.  DPRK  linked   entities  have  used  stable  coins  to   purchase  military  equipment  and  raw   materials  for  munitions.  As  the  report's   authors  explain,  quote,  "This  activity   indicates  an  apparent  effort  by  DPRK   entities  to  operationalize  the  use  of   stable  coins  in  weapons  of  mass   destruction  related  procurement   activities.  Yikes."  The  authors  add  that   stable  coin  transactions  quote  provide   UN  designated  DPRK  entities  with  an   additional  mechanism  to  evade   international  sanctions.  These  illicit   activities  are  often  conducted  using   stable  coins,  usually  USDT,  before  being   exchanged  OTC  for  cash.  The  report  also   highlights  that  Iranian  entities  are   using  stable  coins  to  fund  their   operations  with  one  entity  receiving   several  billion  dollars  in  crypto  in   2024  and  2025.  You'll  have  to  dy  there   if  you  want  the  specifics.  Regardless,   some  Iranian  officials  reportedly  used   crypto  to  purchase  weapons,  drone   components,  and  other  high-tech   equipment,  while  others  sent  stable   coins  to  actors  sanctioned  by  the  UN.   Notably  though,  the  report  points  out   that  even  if  these  stable  coins  were   frozen,  bad  actors  would  likely  switch   to  decentralized  stable  coin   alternatives  like  D  or  USDS  as  it's  now   known  since  they  can't  be  frozen  the   same  way.  Next,  the  report  turns  to   money  laundering.  The  authors  note  that   bad  actors  have  been  using  stable  coins   to  do  investment  fraud,  impersonation   fraud,  romance  scams,  pig  butchering,   and  sextortion.  They  note  that  USDT  and   USDC  are  also  often  used  to  pay  for   synthetic  drugs  from  overseas  suppliers.   These  stable  coins  are  often  converted   to  fiat  and  used  to  reinvest  in  further   drug  production.  Interestingly,  drug   organizations  also  use  stable  coins  to   exploit  high  volumes  in  crypto  casinos   and  merchant  refund  loops.  Basically,   buying  goods  with  stolen  IDs  and   returning  them  for  stable  coin  refunds.   However,  the  authors  add  that   professional  launderers  employ  advanced   tactics  like  chain  hopping,  smurfing,   essentially  breaking  transactions  into   smaller  amounts,  and  cross-platform   transfers.  The  report  then  provides  a   concrete  example  of  how  criminals  use   crypto  and  how  this  gets  detected.  An   Indian  VASP  noted  customer  IP  addresses   tracing  back  to  Southeast  Asia.  These   accounts  were  repeatedly  funded  with   USDT  which  was  immediately  liquidated   and  withdrawn.  But  suspiciously  these   were  the  only  transactions  the  accounts   ever  conducted.  So  the  VASP  tried  to   contact  these  customers  but  couldn't   reach  them  directly.  Eventually  21   people  were  contacted  via  messaging.   Initially  it  looked  like  low  paid  jobs   in  Cambodia  and  Myanmar  being  paid  in   USDT.  However,  the  data  revealed  a   darker  reality.  Multiple  accounts  shared   devices  or  IPs.  Hundreds  of  login  came   from  a  known  scam  compound,  and  some   users  had  been  reported  missing  for   months.  It  turned  out  these  individuals   were  coerced  into  working  at  scam   centers  and  paid  in  cash.  That  cash  was   then  converted  into  crypto  and  sent   overseas  to  friends  and  relatives.  Now,   in  response,  India's  financial   intelligence  unit  took  compliance  action   in  October  last  year  against  the   Southeast  Asia-based  VASP,  which  was   found  to  be  operating  illegally  in   India.  Scary  stuff,  but  not  as  scary  as   the  role  of  stable  coins  in  terrorist   financing.  The  authors  point  out  that   groups  like  ISIS  have  received  stable   coin  donations  or  have  used  stable  coins   to  transfer  funds  internally.  These   groups  rotate  wallet  addresses  to  stay   hidden  and  often  break  transfers  down   into  smaller  amounts  through  multiple   VASPs  to  avoid  suspicion.  The  report   also  notes  that  terrorists  often  combine   stable  coins  with  other  funding  methods,   including  fiat  and  other   cryptocurrencies.  They've  also  been   observed  using  recycled  QR  codes  and  web   domains  even  after  repeated  takedowns.   Now,  more  often  than  not,  illicit  stable   coins  are  rooted  through  unregulated   VASPs  and  laundered  via  mixers,  dexes,   peer-to-peer  or  peer-to-peer  platforms,   exchanges  without  KYC,  or  just  cashed   out  over  the  counter.  In  some  cases,   criminals  even  used  underground  banking   arrangements.  Basically,  if  there's  a   way  to  obscure  a  transaction's  origin,   then  the  bad  guys  are  using  it.  Okay.   The  report  next  examines  stable  coin   vulnerabilities  across  all  three  stages   of  their  life  cycle.  Issuance,   circulation,  and  redemption.  Starting   with  issuance,  the  authors  note  that   stable  coins  borderless  nature  can   encourage  issuers  to  locate  offshore   where  regulatory  frameworks  are  weaker   than  in  the  US.  This  creates  challenges   for  regulators  who  face  quote  regulatory   arbitrage  and  supervisory  blind  spots.   Another  issue  involves  so-called   multi-issuance  stable  coin  schemes.   Basically,  this  is  when  two  or  more   companies  in  different  countries   collaborate  to  issue  one  stable  coin.   The  problem  here  is  that  coins  issued  by   company  A  are  identical  to  those  issued   by  company  B,  creating  regulatory   headaches.  Not  only  does  it  become   unclear  who's  responsible  if  the  stable   coin  is  used  for  illicit  activity,  but   tracing  criminal  funds  also  becomes  far   more  difficult  because  KYC  is  split   between  two  entities.  In  turn,  this   makes  it  much  harder  for  authorities  to   track,  freeze,  or  stop  this  criminal   funding,  especially  since  VPNs  are  often   involved.  Problems  also  arise  during  a   stable  coin  second  stage,  active   circulation.  That's  because  the  market   relies  on  VASP's  integrity  and   regulatory  compliance  to  enforce  AML  and   CFT  measures.  In  practice  though,   compliance  varies  widely  across   different  platforms.  Some  have  weaker   controls  than  others,  while  some   platforms  have  no  compliance  measures  at   all.  And  this  brings  us  to  stage  three,   the  redemption  phase.  The  report  notes   that  the  FATF  requires  any  VASPs  or   financial  institutions  providing   redemption  services  to  remain  fully   compliant  with  AML  and  CFT  laws.   Notably,  these  entities  must  quote   collect  customer  information,  conduct   sanction  screening,  and  comply  with  the   travel  rule  where  applicable  along  with   other  compliance  obligations.  Moreover,   the  FATF  argues  that  this  is  also  why   these  entities  quote  should  therefore  be   subject  to  effective  supervision  with   appropriate  information  available  to   competent  authorities.  So  much  for   financial  privacy.  In  any  case,  the   report  also  notes  that  stable  coin   holders  aren't  required  to  use  official   channels  to  convert  crypto  into  fiat.   Instead,  they  can  use  unlicensed   brokers,  P2P  platforms,  or  other   informal  methods,  completely  bypassing   AML  rules.  This  creates  a  loophole  for   criminals  to  convert  crypto  into  cash  or   other  assets,  making  illicit  activity   much  harder  to  track.  Then  the  report   turns  to  P2P  transactions  using  unhosted   wallets,  wallets  you  control  rather  than   an  exchange.  The  authors  note  that   direct  P2P  transactions  pose  risks   because  they  bypass  regulated   intermediaries  like  VASPs  or  financial   institutions  leaving  them  outside  the   scope  of  laws  preventing  money   laundering,  terrorist  financing  or   proliferation  financing.  These  risks  are   amplified  in  crossber  payments.  That's   because  authorities  find  them  harder  to   monitor  and  their  near  instant   settlement  leaves  little  time  to   intercept  illicit  transactions  if   needed.  Additionally,  the  ability  to   create  new  wallet  addresses  makes  it   difficult  to  track  P2P  transactions  or   determine  if  they  involve  illicit   activity  or  sanctioned  entities.   Now,  to  address  this,  the  FATF  proposes   guidance  for  digital  assets  and  VASPs.   Essentially,  all  VASPs  should  collect   information  on  both  the  sender  and   receiver,  even  for  unhosted  wallets,  and   implement  riskbased  mitigation  measures.   While  this  doesn't  completely  eliminate   the  risks,  it  does  add  a  control  layer   to  help  authorities  trace  and  manage   threats  in  the  largely  unregulated  P2P   stablecoin  ecosystem.  The  report  then   examines  the  vulnerabilities  of   blockchains  themselves.  The  authors  note   that  developers  today  prioritize  stable   coin  interoperability.  As  you'll  know,   this  allows  stable  coins  to  function   across  multiple  blockchains  while   maintaining  the  liquidity  needed  for   crossber  transfers.  However,  criminals   are  increasingly  exploiting  this   interoperability,  moving  stable  coins   across  blockchains  to  fragment   transactions  and  evade  detection.  This   problem  worsens  with  multi-step  chain   hopping  since  blockchains  generally   can't  communicate  with  each  other.  As   such,  freezing  or  blacklisting  assets   becomes  difficult,  especially  when   centralized  stable  coins  are  converted   into  newly  created  wrapped  versions.  The   report  also  notes  that  while  blockchains   provide  transparent  immutable  records,   their  pseudonmous  nature  limits  AML  and   CFT  oversight.  So  the  FATF  emphasizes   collecting  user  information,  including   geographic  data,  as  essential  for  law   enforcement  and  international   cooperation  since  blockchain  data  alone   doesn't  reveal  identities  or  locations.   Moreover,  many  transactions  also  occur   off-chain  within  a  VASP's  internal   ledger,  essentially  bypassing  public   blockchains.   While  off-chain  transfers  are  typically   faster  and  cheaper,  they  also  rely  on   VASPs  to  maintain  customer  data.   However,  unlicensed  or  unregistered   Vasps  don't  always  collect  this   information  or  report  suspicious   activity.  Although  exchanges,  issuers,   and  custodians  can  combine  on  and   off-chain  data  to  detect  suspicious   activity,  regulators  lack  direct  access   to  off-chain  systems.  And  this  creates  a   structural  gap  in  monitoring  illicit   activity,  which  is  why  the  authors   stressed  the  need  for  robust  data   collection,  cooperation  with  VASPs,  and   comprehensive  regulatory  oversight  of   all  stable  coin  transactions.  Okay.  The   next  section  of  the  report  outlines  some   best  practices  to  curb  stable  coin   misuse.  The  authors  recommend  that  all   jurisdictions  enforce  AML  and  CFT   obligations  on  every  VASP  handling   stable  coins,  including  issuers  and   intermediaries.  They  also  stress  that   these  obligations  should  cover  financial   institutions  and  custodians  of  the   reserve  assets  backing  the  stable  coins.   However,  they  also  acknowledged  that  the   stable  coin  ecosystem  is  complex,  making   enforcement  challenging.  In  practice,   only  a  few  jurisdictions  have   regulations  that  distinguish  stable   coins  from  other  cryptocurrencies.   As  such,  the  standards  set  out  by  the   FATF  require  stable  coin  issuers  to   apply  preventative  measures  that  include   customer  due  diligence,  recordkeeping,   suspicious  transaction  reporting,  and   compliance  with  the  travel  rule,  among   others.  Notably,  the  authors  add  that   quote,  "Stable  coin  issuers  should   always  apply  preventative  measures  to   their  customers  in  the  primary  market,   those  who  purchase  or  redeem  the  stable   coin  directly  from  to  the  issuer."  The   FATF  also  advises  jurisdictions  to   consider  how  existing  regulations  could   apply  to  secondary  markets,  including   using  smart  contracts  to  freeze  or  even   burn  assets.   That  said  though,  the  authors   acknowledge  that  while  balances  and   transfers  are  visible  on  chain,  stable   coin  holders  identities  remain  hidden   with  only  pseudonmous  addresses  shown.   While  authorities  can  request  data  from   VASPs,  issuers  often  don't  have  it.   Still,  pseudonmous  addresses  can  be   frozen  via  smart  contracts  if  linked  to   illicit  activity  or  high-risisk   jurisdictions.  With  blockchain   analytics,  wallets  could  theoretically   be  placed  on  an  allow  list,  aka  a  white   list,  to  continue  transacting  or  a  deny   list,  aka  a  blacklist,  to  block  future   activity.  Importantly  though,  the   report's  authors  note  that  blacklists   are  more  commonly  used,  but  typically   reactively  after  illicit  activity  is   detected.  Conversely,  whitelisting   offers  a  more  proactive  approach,  but   requires  knowing  customer  data  in   advance,  which  could  discourage  stable   coin  usage  altogether.  Other  suggested   measures  include  placing  transaction   limits  to  restrict  the  amounts  a  wallet   can  send  per  day,  or  adding  a   requirement  for  pending  transactions  to   be  checked  and  approved  before  being   processed.  They  add  that  this  would   force  compliance  while  allowing  law   enforcement  agencies  to  take  steps  like   confiscating  assets.  However,  many  would   argue  that  this  is  an  overreach.  Not  to   mention  that  it  sounds  eerily  similar  to   the  functionality  of  dystopian  central   bank  digital  currencies  or  CBDC's.  More   on  that  later.  Next  though,  the  report   examines  advanced  tools  for  detecting   and  monitoring  suspicious  transactions,   beginning  with  those  used  by  the  private   sector.  Blockchain  analytics  tools,  for   example,  help  identify  illicit  financing   risks  in  stable  coins  and  have  been   employed  by  the  FATF  and  other   international  bodies.  The  authors  note   that  while  AI,  machine  learning,  and  big   data  have  improved  blockchain  analytics,   these  tools  still  struggle  to  detect   sophisticated  obscuring  techniques.   Moreover,  data  quality  varies  across   platforms,  licenses  can  be  expensive,   and  effective  use  requires  expertise.  As   such,  they  work  best  when  combined  with   other  analytics  investigations  and  human   judgment.  In  the  public  sector,  some   countries  require  stable  coin  issuers  to   use  blockchain  analytics,  while  others   only  recommend  it.  The  FATF  F  notes  that   these  tools  help  identify  suspicious   activity,  but  aren't  sufficient  alone.   Without  strong  compliance  systems  and   clear  regulatory  guidance,  they  cannot   prevent  money  laundering,  terrorist   financing,  or  sanctions  evasion.  The   report  also  stresses  that  regulators   need  strong  technical  knowledge  of   stable  coins  to  regulate  them   effectively.  Specifically,  they  should   be  able  to  assess  whether  existing   controls  adequately  combat  illicit   activity  and  should  collaborate  with  the   private  sector  to  identify  the  most   effective  protections  for  the  stable   coin  ecosystem.  Notably,  some  countries   have  already  proactively  encouraged   cooperation  between  stable  coin   supervisors.  As  such,  the  authors   highlight  supervisory  colleges  as  a  key   best  practice  where  regulators  from   multiple  countries  share  information  and   coordinate  oversight  of  stable  coin   issuers  across  borders.  On  that  note,   the  FATF  also  highlights  that   collaboration  between  the  public  and   private  sectors  is  quote  essential.  By   working  with  the  stablecoin  ecosystem,   the  private  sector  can  better  understand   evolving  trends  and  cooperate  to   strengthen  expertise,  helping  to   maintain  the  integrity  of  the  stable   coin  market.  Then,  when  AML  and  CFT   rules  are  breached  using  stable  coins,   public  private  collaboration  can  enable   decisive  action.  The  authors  note  that   because  transactions  with  unhosted   wallets  are  harder  to  investigate,   investigators  need  the  right  tools  to   track  and  address  illicit  activity.   Specifically,  they  should  be  able  to  use   analytics  tools  to  trace  transactions   end  to  end,  including  on  and  off  ramps,   set  up  alerts  for  flagged  addresses,  and   integrate  onchain  data  into  realworld   investigations.   If  necessary,  centralized  stable  coin   issuers  like  Tether  and  Circle  could   also  be  contacted  to  freeze  suspicious   transactions.  And  finally,  the  FATF   notes  that  although  its  standards  don't   explicitly  cover  unhosted  wallets  or  P2P   transactions,  jurisdictions  should  still   assess  and  mitigate  criminal  risks  in   stable  coins.  The  authors  highlight  that   in  some  jurisdictions,  the  private   sector  has  already  done  just  that.  For   example,  some  VASPs  have  imposed   transfer  limits  on  unhosted  wallets  and   applied  enhanced  KYC  measures.  Some   VASPs  also  use  blockchain  analytics  to   assess  the  risk  of  customers  and  their   counterparties,  implementing  special   measures  to  meet  AML  and  CFT  obligations   for  unhosted  wallets.  In  some  cases,   platforms  have  even  been  prohibited  from   allowing  any  transactions  to  unhosted   wallets  altogether.  All  right,  folks.   That  just  about  wraps  up  the  report  and   remember  you  can  find  the  full  version   in  the  description.  Be  warned  though,  it   is  quite  long.  Anyway,  the  key  takeaway   from  this  report  is  that  the  FATF  is   aiming  to  significantly  ramp  up   regulatory  oversight.  Now,  this  is  a   double-edged  sword.  On  the  one  hand,  it   is  a  good  thing  that  the  FATF  is  putting   more  focus  on  regulatory  compliance   since  this  is  what  will  drive  the   institutional  adoption  of  stable  coins.   Not  to  mention  that  it's  arguably   required  for  the  crypto  market  to  see   mainstream  adoption  and  survive  long   term.  On  the  other  hand,  though,  this   regulatory  focus  comes  at  a  cost.  With   heavy  emphasis  on  KYC  and  freezing   addresses  or  assets,  privacy  is   significantly  reduced.  Many  proposed   measures  resemble  the  functions  of   CBDC's  where  transactions  can  be  traced,   monitored,  limited,  or  even  frozen.  Now,   don't  get  us  wrong.  If  used  the  right   way,  this  could  help  prevent  some  of  the   world's  worst  people  from  committing   heinenous  crimes  that  devastate  lives.   Needless  to  say,  this  would  absolutely   be  a  good  thing.  However,  if  these   systems  are  abused,  then  regulators  and   governments  would  be  given  far  too  much   power.  That's  simply  because  it  could   create  a  dystopian  future  where  stable   coins  are  used  to  control  everything  you   do  with  your  own  money.  What  might  start   with  anti-moneylaundering  controls  could   one  day  end  up  becoming  anti-gambling,   anti-  drinking,  anti-smoking,  or  anti-   anything  that  regulators  see  fit.  In   other  words,  stable  coins  could  be  used   to  track  your  every  move,  putting  an  end   to  your  digital  freedom.  This  is  the   exact  scenario  we're  trying  to  avoid   with  CBDC's,  which  already  have  the   ability  to  do  all  this  by  default.   Thankfully,  the  FatF's  report  doesn't   say  stable  coins  are  inherently  bad,  and   it  does  point  out  that  overregulation   could  dissuade  stable  coin  usage   altogether.  Even  so,  this  is  likely  to   be  a  bitter  pill  for  many  and  could  lead   to  two  outcomes.  First,  more  people   could  turn  to  decentralized  stable  coin   alternatives  like  USDS,  formerly  die.   This  might  push  the  market  share  of   decentralized  stable  coins  well  above   the  10%  noted  at  the  start  of  the   report.  And  second,  this  could  push  more   people  towards  privacy  solutions  to  keep   their  financial  activity  well,  private.   Wanting  your  transactions  to  remain   confidential  doesn't  mean  you're   necessarily  doing  anything  wrong.  It   just  means  you  want  to  transact  freely   without  intermediaries  getting  in  the   way.  And  after  all,  isn't  that  what   crypto  is  meant  to  be  all  about?  Okay,   that's  just  about  all  from  me,  but  we   want  to  hear  from  you  now.  Are  you   worried  about  the  future  of  stable   coins,  or  are  these  measures  long   overdue?  Let  us  know  your  thoughts  in   the  comments  below.  If  you  want  to  see   how  Tether  plans  to  take  on  Circle,  then   check  out  the  video  right  over  here.  And   if  you  want  to  see  how  Wall  Street  is   capturing  crypto,  then  check  out  the   video  over  here.  Okay,  thank  you  all  for   watching  and  I'll  see  you  again  soon.   This  is  Guy  signing  off.
summary_hu: A Financial Action Task Force (FATF) legfrissebb jelentése rávilágít arra, hogy a stabilcoinok egyre népszerűbbé válnak az illegális pénzügyi tranzakciók során, miközben a kiadókat KYC (Know Your Customer) kötelezettségek betartására szólítják fel. A jelentés elemzi a stabilcoinok növekvő piaci részesedését, a bűnözők által alkalmazott technikákat, valamint a pénzmosás és terrorizmus finanszírozásának összefüggéseit. A válaszlépések között szerepel a szigorúbb szabályozás bevezetése, amely azonban csökkentheti a pénzügyi adatvédelmet. Mindezek alapján a jelentés figyelmeztet a stabilcoinok hackingjelenségeire és a decentralizált alternatívák növekvő szerepére. Az igény a pénzügyi adatvédelem iránt nem feltétlenül jelenti a törvények megszegését.
summary_en: The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) recently released a report indicating that stablecoins are increasingly becoming a popular means in illicit financial transactions, prompting calls for issuers to enforce Know Your Customer (KYC) requirements. The report highlights the growing market share of stablecoins, the methodologies employed by criminals, and the connections to money laundering and terrorist financing. Proposed responses include stricter regulations, which may, however, reduce financial privacy. Overall, the report warns of the hacking vulnerabilities associated with stablecoins and the rising significance of decentralized alternatives. The desire for financial privacy does not inherently imply wrongdoing.
key_points_hu: A stabilcoinok szerepe az illegális pénzügyi tranzakciókban növekszik.
A KYC kötelezettségek életbe léptetése csökkentheti a pénzügyi adatvédelmet.
A decentralizált alternatívák piaci részesedése nőhet.
key_points_en: The role of stablecoins in illicit financial transactions is increasing.
The implementation of KYC requirements may reduce financial privacy.
Market share of decentralized alternatives may grow.
